3 Can you see an atom?

1 Heat 1 cup of 3% homogenised milk on a hot plate or in the microwave until it bubbles
2 Add the hot milk to 1 tbsp (15 mL) of vinegar in a large bowl
3 Pour the curdled mixture into a sieve lined with paper towels
4 Allow the white solid to dry in the sieve for ~30 mins. Squeeze the solid in a ball between paper towels to further remove water.
5 Flatten out the dried solid and press it with a cookie cutter.

6 Place the pressed-out shape in the window for 24 hours to harden. You have demonstrated with children that the plastics can be bent and shaped. You have made a natural polymer plastic from a protein found in milk called Casein. The combination of heat and vinegar (acetic acid) helps to pull out or

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Analyse and interpret data on the properties of substances before and after the substances interact to determine if a chemical reaction has occurred.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
1 In a chemical reaction the starting materials are called reactants. (T)
2 In a reaction of vinegar with steel wool we would say that iron oxide (rust) is a product of the reaction. (T)

3 Dissolving solids in water is a physical change. (T) Remind Learners that in grade 5, they would have done several experiments where they cooled substances, melted substances, mixed substances, etc. We learned the difference between a physical change and a chemical change. Let us review, with this
4 A chemical reaction that overall causes cooling is called an exothermic reaction (F)
5 The reaction of iron and oxygen is an exothermic reaction because it creates heat. (T)
6 Combustion is the process of burning (T)

1 Your grandmother is making a loaf bread in the kitchen. What evidence can you provide her that this represents a chemical change and not a physical change? (gas evolved to rise bread, starting materials consumed)

2 The power goes out and you light a wax candle with a match. Through the evening the candle continues to run until the candle ha almost disappeared. What evidence can you provide that burning a candle is a chemical change? (heat is produced, a gas is given off, wax as starting material is consumed)

3 You mistakenly leave a litre of milk out on the counter on a hot day. When you return you notice big changes. What evidence can you provide that a chemical change has occurred? (spoiled odor, curdling- precipitate)

4 Your parent's car will not start. You suspect the battery is the problem. You open the hood and observe the battery cells and terminals. What evidence is likely present that would suggest a chemical change has been occurring that causes the battery to malfunction? (corrosion-terminals may food-des

5 You visited a carnival celebration and observed elaborate fireworks explosions. What evidence can you suggest that fireworks involves chemical reactions? (burning occurs of the reactants, light is produced, gas is produced)
1 Label three plastic cups Soap, Water and Hard Water.
2 Prepare the soap solution (a few drops of liquid soap in a glass of water and stirring the mixture).
3 Prepare the hard water (2 teaspoons of Epsom salt (magnesium sulfate) to a glass of water. Stir the solution until no more solid will dissolve).
4 Place ¼ cup of water in the cup labelled Water.
5 Add 1 tablespoon of the soap solution to both the Water and Hard Water cups.

6 Look at both cups from the top and the side. Ask Learners: ● What differences, if any, do you observe in the way soap combines with water compared with what happened when it was added to hard water?
1 Why do you think some items made from steel will

2 Persons living near to the sea have observed that metal objects tend to rust more easily than those much further away from the sea. Why do you think this is so? (the salt in the air may assist the rust forming reaction)
3 Ask Learners to make a list of items in the community that rust easily. (steel items)
4 What do you think can be done to help to prevent rusting? ( Coating item with a layer of paint of grease)
5 Why do you think certain items are made from stainless steel instead of iron? (Stainless steel less likely to rust)

6 Find the cost of an item that is made from stainless steel and one made from iron? (stainless steel is more costly) One Last Indicator of Chemical Change: Light Burning of a candle or wood is the heating of the reactant to a high enough temperature in the presence of oxygen. This causes what we ca
1 Using a measuring cylinder/a measuring cup/a beaker, measure 100 mL of water
2 Add the 100 mL of water to a bottle and place the bottle on a weighing scale.
3 Place an effervescent tablet in a balloon after breaking it into two or three pieces.
4 Place the balloon with the tablet on the scale with the bottle.
5 Learners should note the total mass of the water, the bottle, balloon and effervescent tablet in their notebooks.
6 Fit the neck of the balloon tightly around the mouth of the bottle (containing the water) being careful not to drop the antacid pieces into the water at the bottom of the bottle.

7 Ask Learners what they think might happen when you raise the balloon above the water bottle and allow the tablet pieces to drop in the bottle? (dissolving of the tablet in water and then a reaction with gas produced)
8 Raise the balloon and allow the tablet pieces to drop into the water. The Learners will see bubbles begin to form and the balloon should partially inflate.
9 Ask Learners what evidence they have that there has been a chemical reaction? (reactant consumed, gas bubbles produced and trapped in balloon)

10 When the tablets have fully dissolved and the carbon dioxide bubbles subside, place the whole system on the weighing scale again. Have Learners record in their notebooks the mass of the system after the chemical reaction (i.e. the products).
1 The balloon will be pushed into the flask as the iron consumes the oxygen and makes iron oxide (rust),
2 The mass of the system, start to finish, does not change.

6 Fit the neck of the balloon tightly around the mouth of the bottle (containing the water) being careful not to drop the antacid pieces into the water at the bottom of the bottle.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Develop models to describe that organisms have unique and diverse life cycles but all have in common birth, growth, reproduction, and death
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Define the following: o life stages o life cycle o offspring o reproduce o metamorphosis o pupa o chrysalis o larva o proboscis o Dengue fever o Zika virus o Yellow fever o Chikungunya o egg

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: See specific curriculum outcomes
specific_curriculum_outcomes: 1 State what is meant by the term life cycle. (It is called a life cycle because it shows the different stages that an organism goes through from birth to reproduction where the cycle begins again).
2 What are the four (4) main stages that any life cycle goes through? (Birth, growth, reproduction and death are the four stages that all animals go through.)

3 The following is a diagram of the life cycle of a butterfly. Label the diagram and explain what is happening at each stage. Life cycles Each living thing undergoes a series of changes as it goes through the different life stages. These changes follow a particular pattern that is very specific to i
1 Have you ever touched a butterfly? (Yes/No)
2 What did it look like? (colors, wing shapes, sizes))
3 Was it colourful? (Yes/No)
4 Some organisms can go through what is termed metamorphosis. It can be complete or incomplete metamorphosis. State the meaning of the following terms:
5 State which of the following organisms undergo complete metamorphosis and which undergo incomplete metamorphosis, by ticking the correct box.
1 Observe and record the development of the caterpillars each day.

2 Record all changes from caterpillar to pupa, to the fully developed butterfly phase. Learners should draw labelled pictures of the stages in their notebooks making note of the timeline of each stage.

3 Learners are expected to use the following materials to create a model of the stages of the butterfly development. Teacher note: Materials which can be used to make models of the life cycle of the butterfly may include paper plates, and coloured pipe cleaners (cut in half)/pieces of wool, small be

6 In their life cycle, human beings go through six major stages. List three of these six stages and state one characteristic of each stage listed. Identify the different parts of the larva (caterpillar) and adult: head, thorax, abdomen, antenna, mouth, eyes, legs, pro-legs, etc. Ask Learners how the

7 List four different characteristics in which variation might be found among the Learners in your class. Possible answers: Height Mass Hand length Foot length Skin colour Hair colour Handedness (Left -handed or right - handed) Head size

8 State three ways in which the life cycle of a frog is different from the life cycle of human beings. (Frogs and humans start as babies, grow and change, become adults, reproduce, and eventually die. However, there are differences in their life cycle. Female frogs lay tiny, soft eggs in standing wa
1 How many parts is its body divided into? (3 parts, the head, thorax, and abdomen.)
2 How many legs does it have? (6 legs)
3 How many wings does it have?(4 wings, two pairs)
4 How many eyes does it have? (2 eyes)
5 How many things are on its head? (2 antennae, eyes, palpi, proboscis)
6 From information available, select a butterfly. What colour is it? (Answer will vary)
7 Does it have a tongue? (The butterfly has a structure called a proboscis that is considered a tongue. However, it looks more like a straw than a tongue. It can be rolled and unrolled)
1 Identify sites in and around the home that mosquitoes may breed in.
2 Look around the home to see what is being done by your parents and siblings to control mosquitoes
3 What are three things that can be done by you and your family members to reduce mosquitoes?
1 Where did the caterpillar come from? (an egg)
2 How does the caterpillar meet its own needs? (It meets its needs from the plant it feeds on)
3 How does a caterpillar grow and change? As the caterpillar eats, it gets larger and larger until it becomes too large for its skin and moults to make new skin)
4 How are caterpillars and butterflies different? (Different body parts, shape, colour, caterpillar crawls while the butterfly flies)
5 Which adult butterfly lays eggs?(Female)
6 Why is it called a life cycle? (It is called a life cycle because it shows the different stages that an organism goes through from birth to reproduction where the cycle begins again)
7 What does the caterpillar do in the chrysalis? (inside the cocoon, the caterpillar changes into a wormlike creature called a pupa or chrysalis)
1 Collect the different stages of the mosquito life cycle: Show the children examples of eggs, pupa, larva and adult.
2 Keep the different stages in a large screw top jar or an aquarium with a glass top. Put water weeds in the jar or aquarium.
3 Teach the children to identify the different stages in the life cycle of the mosquito and draw labelled pictures of the stages in their notebooks.
1 How long their animal takes care of its babies, if at all? Why this is necessary.
2 What their animal do to take care of its babies?
3 Identify the animal when it is very young and when the animal is older.
4 Describe the changes they see between the different stages of the animal's life cycle.
5 How does the life cycle of the animal compare to that of a human being?
6 After Learners have completed their searches/ readings, ask Learners to come together as a class and have each group present their findings. Prompts for discussion could include:

Teacher Note: It is important that Learners are able to recognize stages in the life cycle of the mosquito, as these insects can transmit diseases such as D

2 Which is the least common shoe length in the class
3 Which is the most common shoe length in the class?
4 Which is the least common shoe length in the class Recognizing mosquito life cycle at home/ Controlling Mosquitos

1 Show the children diagrams of the Aedes aegypti mosquito that carries dengue fever. Point out to them that this is the same mosquito that spreads Zika and Chikungunya viruses. Allow them time to observe the diagrams.
2 Encourage the children to collect stages of the life cycle of the mosquito found near their homes.
3 Discuss areas in and around the home and in the community that may be good breeding sites for mosquitoes.

4 Discuss what can be done to control mosquitoes near homes and in the community. Control Mosquitoes carry diseases such as malaria fever, dengue fever, encephalitis, and the Ross River virus. The larva is the stage when you can most easily control the mosquito, because it has to live in water for a
5 What was the size of the largest/smallest hand, etc. that you measured?
6 What were the differences in hand sizes between people of the same age?
7 What was the difference between the smallest and largest head size? Activity: Drawing and describing the different stages of a plant's life cycle
1 Print diagrams showing the different stages of a plant's life cycle.

2 Ask Learners to label each stage and explain what is happening at each stage. Homework Assignment Look around your yard, select five plants and state what stage of their lifecycle they are at. Activity: Creating an art piece of a stage of the life cycle of a flowering plant In this activity, Learn
1 Gather frog eggs and place them in an aquarium and observe each day. Tadpoles hatch from frog eggs in a week to ten days and the process from tadpole to frog is fascinating to observe.
2 A frog lays eggs; they hatch into tadpoles, then grow into frogs. You can draw a picture on the board of a complete cycle of an animal of your choice.
3 Fertilised frog eggs are usually easy to find.
4 Place them in an aquarium and observe each day. Tadpoles hatch from frog eggs in a week to ten days and the process from tadpole to frog is fascinating to observe.
5 Learners should draw in their notebooks, a labelled picture of the stages be sure to include a timeline of the development.
6 As Learners make their observations, they should answer the following question sin their notebooks:

1 At which stage or stages of its life cycle is a frog most like a fish? Explain your answer. (Tadpole stage) (When the tadpole hatches, it looks more like a fish than a frog. It doesn't have any legs and it has gills that allow it to breathe underwater).
2 At which stage does it look like its half frog and half fish? (Young frog or froglet)
3 At which stage does it look like a frog? (It looks like a frog when it gets to the final stage or an adult frog.)
1 Make sure you have a powdered donut and a plain donut in front of you.
2 Put your hands behind your back.
3 Take a bite of your powdered donut without using your hands.
4 Without wiping your mouth, take a bite of your plain donut (again, without using your hand)
5 Observe your plain doughnut. Questions:
1 What do you observe about your plain donut? (It now has powdered sugar, from the other doughnut.)
2 Discuss with Learners how this exercise compares with actual pollination involving pollinators and flowers.
1 For this assignment, each student will design a flower using construction paper, pipe cleaners and other craft materials, or using a computer software

2 Have them describe how it would be pollinated (by an animal, wind, or humans) and how the features of the flower help in the pollination process. Learners will present their flower to the rest of the class. Presenters will name their flower, describe it and state how it is Frog
1 How is the life cycle of the cockroach similar and different from the life cycle of a butterfly? (time frame of life cycle)
2 Which areas of the home do you think you will find cockroach eggs? (dark, warm & damp areas)
3 The cockroach is a pest, what are some of the reasons why it is difficult to control? (fast insects/sensitive to vibrations/known to develop resistance to insecticides)
1 Foetus -At this time, a baby is growing inside its mum's womb. During this stage, the foetus is entirely dependent on the female for everything
2 Baby - A baby is born after spending nine months inside the womb
3 Childhood -At this stage, the child learns to walk, talk and eat independently.
4 Adolescence - The stage between childhood and adulthood when children become teenagers. Here, bodies develop during adolescence.
5 Adulthood - At this stage, the human body is fully developed and this is usually the stage where reproduction usually takes place.
6 This is the last stage in the lifecycle of a human. This period starts from 65 years of age, until the end of their lives.

1 Make a list of the plants with brightly coloured flowers. Take as many pictures of the pictures of the flowers as possible. Make sketches of these flowers if you do not have a camera. Note their colour and size.
2 Make a note of the insects or birds that visit these flowers. Find out the names of these organisms with your teacher's help.
3 Try to catch a few of the insect pollinators, using a net, not your hands. See if a yellow powdery substance is sticking to their legs and bodies. This substance is pollen (pollen grains).
4 Take as many pictures as possible of the organisms visiting the flowers.
5 When Learners return to the classroom, discuss their findings with them. Make a list of the pollinators that were seen and the flowers they visited. After the class discussion:
1 Assign each group a pollinator. Provide the group with a Profile card that corresponds to the pollinator that they were assigned.
2 Pass out the Pollinator Observation Data Sheet.
3 Learners will then return to the area they first visited and use the data sheet on their pollinator to find out the following:
4 What number of flowers did your pollinator visit the most? (Answers will vary.)
5 What are three flower traits that you think attracted your pollinator? (Answers will vary.) Other questions
1 Why do pollinators visit flowers? (Pollinators visit flowers in search of nectar and pollen, to use as food).
2 How do pollinators know which flowers to go to? (There are many different ways pollinators decide which flowers to visit, these include the colour, taste, patterns and popularity of the flower.)

3 What are two strategies that flowers have to attract the most pollinators? (Many flowers use colours to attract insects, sometimes helped by coloured marks to guide them. Flowers are often shaped to provide a landing platform for visiting insects to land on or to force them to brush against anther
1 Why do you think the petals, the stamen and the stigma fall off? (They fall off because they are no longer needed.)

2 Why is it important to protect the embryo? (It is necessary to protect the embryo from drying out and from any injuries. If the embryo is damaged, it will not be able to germinate and produce a new plant.)

3 Give 2 examples of fruits that contain 1 seed, a few seed and many seeds (Examples of fruits that contain one seed are: Avocado pear, Coconut, Mango, Plum. Examples of fruits that contain a few seeds are: Sapodilla, Grape, Apple, Grapefruit Examples of fruits that contain many seeds are: Watermelo
1 Have Learners trace the path of the pollen and label where fertilization happens on their diagrams.
2 Observe the stamen (with anthers and filament) and the small, thin, pistil. Do you see pollen? (Yes/No)
3 There is an anther cap; pollen is not released until the flower is ready. How is this pistil different from the diagrams you have seen? (Answers will depend on the type of flower used).
4 How many stamens are there? (Answers will depend on the type of flower used).
5 Draw the stamens and pistils in your notebook and label them.
6 Gently pull out each of the stamens. Only a pistil and ovary (the bottom bulb-like structure) will remain. Dissect the ovary.
1 With your toothpick, make a vertical slice down the middle of the ovary and open it like a book.

2 Place the two halves of the ovary on your plate. What do you see inside the ovary? What do you notice about the way ovules are arranged? What needs to happen so ovules grow into seeds? Draw your dissected ovary with ovules in your notebook and label them. Use your hand lens to see the arrangement
3 Indicate the location of pollen grains and ovules on a diagram of a flower and trace the path from the stigma to the ovary of a flower.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Use mathematical representations to describe a simple model for waves that includes how the amplitude of a wave is related to the energy in a wave.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.

Define the terms: ○ Wave ○ Mechanical wave ○ Electromagnetic wave ○ Energy ○ Amplitude ○ Wavelength ○ Frequency ○ Trough ○ Crest ○ Vibration ○ Longitudinal waves ○ Transverse waves ○ SONAR Examples of Waves Learners give examples that show they know the difference between mechanical waves and electr
Explain the relationship between amplitude and energy of a wave
Explain the mathematical formula that relates amplitude to energy of a wave
Calculate the energy of a wave given its amplitude, using an appropriate mathematical formula
Interpret a graph of wave function, showing how energy is related to the amplitude of a wave
Predict how changes in amplitude will affect the energy of a wave
Learners will develop interest and curiosity about wave behavior
Learners will value the importance of mathematical modeling in interpreting wave behavior
Learners will demonstrate respect for peers during group work activities and discussions
Learners will demonstrate the utility of waves in everyday technologies.
We don't have terms to describe parts of the wave.

We are not sure which measurements to make. So class, it seems that having a "language" in science, one that labels particular parts of objects, could be very useful We see different types of waves everyday in our world, with new terms we can more easily describe them.
Accuracy- Poster accurately explains how amplitude affects energy (5 marks)
Clarity-Poster is visually clear and well organized with appropriate pictures/graphics (5 marks)
Creativity-Poster demonstrates originality (5 marks)
Grammar & punctuation (2 marks)
Calculate the square of each amplitude value (4 marks)
Plot amplitude squared values against energy. (4 marks)
Draw a line of best fit which should indicate a linear relationship between the square of the amplitude and energy. (1 mark)

Learners should discuss why the rice moves more with higher amplitude taps and how the results show that energy is directly proportional to the square of the amplitude.(6 marks) (Amplitude is the maximum distance from the rest of a wave particle. When a balloon drum is tapped from a greater height,
How does a change in amplitude affect the sound energy produced by the drum? (Higher amplitude vibrations result in an increase in sound energy and therefore louder sounds)
Longitudinal waves are a type of mechanical wave that moves parallel (same direction) to the medium they travel through, like sound waves in air or spring waves in metal.

Transversal waves are waves that oscillate perpendicular to the direction of a wave's advance. This means that the particles of the medium vibrate up and down or side to side, while the wave travels forward. Examples of transverse waves include surface ripples on water and electromagnetic waves such
Sound is amplified the most (loudest) through solids as the particles are closest together.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Develop and use a model to describe that waves are reflected, absorbed, or transmitted through various materials.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
1 Mirror-Bright flashlight beam and their reflections are observed. This is because the mirror has a smooth, shiny surface which causes light to be bounced back or be reflected.
2 Transparent plastic-Objects behind the plastic become illuminated and can be seen. This is because the plastic is transparent, allowing most light to pass through or be transmitted.
3 Black paper-The paper appears dark and nothing behind it can be seen. After a while the paper may feel warm This is because the dark surface absorbs

1 Explain why sound waves are mechanical waves rather than electromagnetic waves. Sound waves are mechanical waves because they require a medium (such as air, water, or solid materials) to travel through. They are created by vibrations that cause particles in the medium to move and transfer energy.

2 Cathy yells in a tunnel and hears her voice echo. Is this an example of transmission, absorption, or reflection? Explain. This is an example of reflection. The sound waves produced by Cathy's yell bounce off the walls most of the light, converting it to heat, and very little is reflected to the ob

3 When Gordon jumps into the pool, he creates waves that travel from one end of the pool to the other. Is this an example of transmission, absorption, or reflection? Explain. This is an example of transmission. The waves generated by Gordon's jump travel through the water from one end of the pool to

Before Nick had carpet installed in his bedroom, Justin could hear the sound of Nick singing as he practiced for chorus class each night. Once Nick's room was carpeted, Justin could no longer hear Nick singing. Why

Describe the interactions occurring between the sound waves and the wall. The sound waves from Jennifer's guitar are partially transmitted throug

The green color of the leaves is due to the absorption and reflection of light waves. The leaves contain chloro

Describe the interactions occurring between the light waves and the window. The pattern of light and dark areas is caused by transmission and sh

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Apply Newton's Third Law to design a solution to a problem involving the motion of two colliding objects.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.

1 What did you observe once the astronaut applied a force to the basketball in microgravity? (due to the difference in masses, the basketball accelerated much faster than the astronaut although an equal and opposite force was applied)
2 What did you observe once the astronaut applied a force to an astronaut of a similar mass? (both astronauts accelerated in opposite directions)
1 State Newton's third law (for every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction)
2 Describe the action and reaction forces of a rocket being launched (the rocket exhaust moves downwards while the rocket moves upwards)
3 Describe the action and reaction forces of a cannon being fired (the cannon's backward movement is the reaction to the action of shooting a cannonball)
4 What did Newton observe about the magnitude of action and reaction forces? (action and reaction forces are always equal)
5 What is the magnitude of the reaction force if a skater pushes off the ground with 15 Newtons of force? (the ground pushes back with a reaction force of 15N)
6 Describe the effect of friction on a moving body (frictional forces would move in the opposite direction of the moving body causing more energy to be used)
7 Describe three examples of action and reaction forces which you may encounter in your everyday life.
1 What did the cyclist do to get the bicycle to move? (He had to use force to pedal the bicycles.)
2 How did the lawnmower get the machine to move? (He had to push it forward.)
3 How did the boy get the stationary ball to move? (The stationary ball moved after the boy had kicked it.)
4 How did the baker get the desired shape he wanted using the dough? (he applied force by kneading it)
1 Moving a stationary object: When a force is applied to an object that is not moving, it can make
2 Stopping a moving object: Force can also be used to stop an object that is already in motion. Imagine applying brakes to a bicycle or a car to slow it down and bring it to a stop.

3 Changing the speed of a moving object: When a force is applied to an object that is already moving, it can either make it go faster or slow it down. For example, pushing a swing can make it move faster while using the brakes on a bicycle can make it slow down.

4 Changing the direction of a moving object: If a force is applied to an object at an angle to its current path, it can cause the object to change its direction. Think of kicking a soccer ball to make it go in a different direction.
5 Changing the shape and size of an object: Forces can also cause objects to change their shape or size. For instance, if you squeeze a soft ball, it will temporarily change its shape.
1 An electromagnet can generate a stronger magnetic field than a regular magnet. (T)
2 I would expect the electromagnet's strength to depend on the size (voltage) of the battery because of the current flow generated. (T)
3 The number of windings on the nail had no impact on the magnetic attraction of the paper clips. (F)
4 Electromagnets at junk yards require low voltages to lift heavy items like cars. (F)
5 Electromagnets are very useful because they can be turned on and off. (T)

2 Maglev trains Primary Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8os4 A4li674 (5:39 mins)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Plan an investigation to provide evidence that the change in an object's motion depends on the sum of the forces on the object and the mass of the object.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: 1 What did the cyclist do to get the bicycle to move? (He had to use force to pedal the bicycles.)
2 How did the lawnmower get the machine to move? (He had to push it forward.)
3 How did the boy get the stationary ball to move? (The stationary ball moved after the boy had kicked it.)
4 How did the baker get the desired shape he wanted using the dough? (he applied force by kneading it)
1 Moving a stationary object: When a force is applied to an object that is not moving, it can make
2 Stopping a moving object: Force can also be used to stop an object that is already in motion. Imagine applying brakes to a bicycle or a car to slow it down and bring it to a stop.

3 Changing the speed of a moving object: When a force is applied to an object that is already moving, it can either make it go faster or slow it down. For example, pushing a swing can make it move faster while using the brakes on a bicycle can make it slow down.

4 Changing the direction of a moving object: If a force is applied to an object at an angle to its current path, it can cause the object to change its direction. Think of kicking a soccer ball to make it go in a different direction.
5 Changing the shape and size of an object: Forces can also cause objects to change their shape or size. For instance, if you squeeze a soft ball, it will temporarily change its shape.
1 An electromagnet can generate a stronger magnetic field than a regular magnet. (T)
2 I would expect the electromagnet's strength to depend on the size (voltage) of the battery because of the current flow generated. (T)
3 The number of windings on the nail had no impact on the magnetic attraction of the paper clips. (F)
4 Electromagnets at junk yards require low voltages to lift heavy items like cars. (F)
5 Electromagnets are very useful because they can be turned on and off. (T)
1 Electric motors Primary source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CW ulQ1ZSE3c (10:03 mins)
2 Maglev trains Primary Source: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=8os4 A4li674 (5:39 mins)

specific_curriculum_outcomes: 1 An electromagnet can generate a stronger magnetic field than a regular magnet. (T)
2 I would expect the electromagnet's strength to depend on the size (voltage) of the battery because of the current flow generated. (T)

strands.strand_name: Matter and Energy in Organisms and Ecosystems

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Analyse and interpret data to provide evidence for the effects of resource availability on organisms and populations of organisms in an ecosystem
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.

1 Name two predators in the food web. Introductory Activity: Learners, do you remember the definition of a food web? (A food web shows the feeding relationship between living things. It is made up of food chains connected together).
2 Name two prey animals from the food web. (grasshopper, mouse, rabbit)
3 Which animals are competing to eat the grasshopper? (sparrow and owl) (Sparrow feeds on the grasshopper or the owl feeds on the grasshopper).
4 What would happen to the population of sparrows if all the grasshoppers died out? (the population of sparrows would decrease)
1 Identify the producers and consumers in the food webs.
2 Analyze a food web and make predictions about outcomes, if there is a breakdown at any stage in the food web.
3 Suggest what would happen to the organisms in the food web if: (a) All the insect larvae died out. (b) Human overharvest the large fishes (c) Rapid increase in water beetles.
1 What happens to the population of hawks when the population of field mice increases? (The population of the hawks increases.)
2 Why do you think this happens? (There is more food for the hawks.)
3 What happens to the population of field mice when the population of hawks increases? (When the population of hawks increases the population of the mice decreases.)
4 How would you describe the relationship between these two populations? (An increase of the hawk population has a negative effect on the population of mice.
1 What are the two birds competing for? (bread - food)
2 Which bird in the picture has the advantage? why?( the pigeon because it is bigger)
3 What else do animals compete for? ( animals often compete for food but also compete for space, water, mate, shelter)
1 For each of the examples shown, suggest what the animals or plants are competing for. (Sea snail and the clown fish both compete for the plankton).
2 Think of two more examples of living things (animals or plants) competing for resources.
1 Make a bird feeder station by hanging up seeds. Sprinkle different bird seeds onto the tray on the ground.
2 Record the number of birds that visit the feeding station. Do this for ten minutes in the morning, ten minutes at midday and ten minutes in the afternoon.
3 Analyse your results. (a) Did some birds only take one type of food? (b) Did you see any competition for food?
1 Which animals are competing to eat the grasshopper? (bird, owl) These animals both eat the grasshopper. Therefore, they are competing for the same source of food.
2 What do you notice about the arrows when animals are competing in a food web? (Competition is shown in a food web when the arrows point from a food source to more than one consumer.)
1 Predict the population of rabbits in 2012. Modify the model to include your prediction.

2 Suggest and explain a factor that may cause the drop-in population of grass and rabbits. compete for attention. In this instance, both of you are working against each other to try to get what you want. Just like humans, animals also compete in nature for resources. What resources do you think anim

3 Suggest an organism that shares the same niche as the rabbit and explain how its introduction into the ecosystem would impact the rabbit population. Source:https://docs.google.com/document/d/1RG19rk5ksM 5dRjZXVffZ4P8Rh0yP7ibQ- 1TDbMte22w/template/preview
1 Season of heavy rains flood an area and create new ponds - algae begin to grow rapidly at the surface of the water,
2 A hurricane brings salt water from the ocean into rivers and streams. - The larger fish begin to fight among themselves for smaller prey.
3 Humans cut down many trees in a forest - ferns and grasses reach outward into the open sunshine.

4 A fire moves through a grassland ecosystem. - Prairie dog burrows are inhabited by other savanna mammals. retrieved from: https://www.othelloschools.org/cms/lib/WA022155 24/Centricity/Domain/213/7th%20Week%202%20 ELA.pdf
1 Scarcity: not enough of something
2 Abundance: having more than enough of something.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Construct an explanation that predicts patterns of interactions among organisms across multiple ecosystems.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Grade 6 Science Curriculum
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document
1 A relationship between two organisms in which one is harmed and the other benefits is called ______________.
2 A relationship in which two different organisms live together is called ______________.
3 Relationship where one organism hunting, killing and eating another organism is called __________.
4 A relationship between two organisms in which both organisms benefit is called ________.
5 When an animal is hunted and killed for food it is called the _________.
6 When an animal is the hunter and kills for food it is called the ___________.
1 Parasitism 2. Symbiosis 3. Predator/Prey
4 Mutualism 5. Prey 6. Predator Retrieved from https://www.slideserve.com/airell/use-the- following-words-to-fill-in-the-blanks-a-prey-c- parasitism-e-mutualism

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Construct and interpret graphical displays of data to describe the relationships of kinetic energy to the mass of an object and to the speed of an object.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
1 Kinetic energy and mass (Ans: B)
2 Kinetic energy and velocity (Ans: A)
1 Kinetic energy is the energy of objects that are in motion.
2 Potential energy is stored energy, ready to be converted into another form
3 As the mass of an object increases, the kinetic energy of the object increases.
4 As the speed of an object increases, the kinetic energy of the object increases.
5 Mass and kinetic energy have a direct or proportional relationship.
1 multiply his mass (m) by 1 2: 50 ✕ 1 2 = 25
2 square the speed/velocity (v): 102 = 100
3 Multiply the answers in 1 and 2 to get the Kinetic Energy of the boy/girl on a bicycle going down the hill:
1 What type of energy does the wheel possess before it is released?(potential energy)
2 What type of energy does the wheel possess as it rolls down?(kinetic energy)
3 Does the wheel move at the same speed if it is released from halfway point? Give a reason for your answer ( No, the potential energy of the wheel is less since the height is reduced).
1 Divide the class into teams of three or four Learners each.
2 Show the Learners how to use a straw like a water dropper: Stick the straw in a glass of water. Place a finger on the top of the straw. Pull the straw out of the glass, while
3 Ask the Learners how they can all be sure to use the same amount of water for each trial. (Answer: By marking a line on the straw and always refilling it to that point.)

4 Discuss with Learners' methods for measuring the diameter of a splash. Have the Learners agree on a standard class method for determining the diameter of a splash. Have Learners use a pencil or pen to outline the splash, and label it with their name, the drop height, and the splash diameter.

5 Have student teams describe on the worksheet what they predict will happen to the splash as it is dropped from increasing heights. Do they expect the size of the splash to increase or decrease with higher drop heights?

6 Have the class agree on four heights from which to drop the water, such as 30 cm, 60 cm, 90 cm and 1 m (note this on the worksheet). Teams measure the diameter of the water splash for all four heights. Each team member drops the water once from each height. Thus, teams with three members have 12 d
7 Learners take turns doing each part of the activity. One student holds the meter stick. One student drops the water using the

8 Hold the bottom of the straw at the height by first placing it next to the correct height on the meter stick and then moving it several centimeters away, being careful to keep the straw at the same height. On the paper, write the height, diameter and team member name, and record the data on the wo
9 After one person drops the water, the team members switch places and repeat the process. The student measuring the diameter of the splash moves the paper so the water falls on a dry piece.
10 After teams have finished their drops, have them complete the worksheet by calculating averages and creating a graph of their average data.

11 Conclude with a class-wide comparison of each team's results (table data and graphs) and a concluding discussion, as described in the Assessment section. Source: Falling Water - Activity - TeachEngineering https://www.teachengineering.org/activities/view /cub_energy2_lesson08_activity1
1 As the distance from the surface increases, the gravitational potential energy of the liquid increases.
2 This increase in potential energy is directly related to the distance the particles move when they hit the surface (splash diameter).
3 The potential energy is converted to kinetic energy according to the principle of conservation of energy.

2012 1. How does the design maximize heat transfer by: a. Conduction? copper tubing b. Convection? Cold water at the bottom of the collector, hot water at the top. c. Radiation? Black surface at the bottom of the collector and tubes are enclosed in glass (greenhouse effect).

2 Why is the cold water tap placed at the bottom of the tank? Cold water is denser than hot water. The top of the tank will have the warmest water and we do not want the cold water to cool it down in the storage tank.
3 Why is the hot water tap placed at the top of the tank? Hot water is less dense that cold water so the hottest water will always rise to the top of the tank.

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Analyse and interpret data to determine scale properties of objects in the solar system.
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
1 Cut 10 strings each 3 meters in length. These strings each represent 500 x 106 km of distance (that is 500,000,000 km). Give a string to each group for their respective planet.
2 Refer to the following table. It shows the distances in our solar system. Share this with Learners and explain that ten to the power of six essentially means 6 zeros added to the number.

3 With the teacher representing the sun, each celestial body group will use their string to measure how far their celestial body will be from the sun. Keep in mind the distance may be a fraction of the string or a multiple of the string length.

1 Size Comparison: Have Learners use the diameters of planets to scale their models. For example, if Earth is 12,742 km and you choose a smaller scale, like 1 cm = 1,000 km, Earth would be about 12.74 cm in diameter.
2 Distance calculations: Using the scale and data for the actual distances between the planets and the Sun, calculate how far each planet should be from the Sun in the model.

1 What is one reason Earth can support life? a. Earth is the largest planet in the solar system. b. Earth has water in all three forms (solid, liquid, gas). c. Earth is closest to the Sun. d. Earth has the shortest day/night cycle.

4 Were Learners surprised, the planets are not evenly spaced, some are very close in a group and others are very far away. Remind them that Pluto has recently been removed from the designation of planet. The outer planets are very cold because they are so distant from the sun. See distance activity
2 Which of the following is essential for living organisms to survive on Earth? a. Extreme temperatures b. A thick layer of ice c. An atmosphere containing oxygen d. No atmosphere at all Answer: c

3 How does Earth's atmosphere protect life? a. It keeps water vapour in space. b. It reflects all sunlight away from Earth. c. It blocks harmful radiation from the Sun. d. It makes Earth the hottest planet. Answer: c

1 Explain why liquid water is necessary for life on Earth. (Water is essential because all living organisms need it for survival. It helps with biological processes such as transporting nutrients and removing waste.)

2 How does Earth's distance from the Sun contribute to supporting life? (Earth's distance from the Sun places it in the "habitable zone," where temperatures are just right for liquid water to exist. It's not too hot or too cold for life.)

3 What role does the atmosphere play in keeping Earth's temperatures stable for life? word cancel and instead say mins/mins = 1 > anything multiplied by one is itself! 3 x 108 m/s x 3.1536 x 107 seconds/yr = 9.5 x 1015m/yr Point out that the exponents of 10 are additive here.
1 Which is the biggest planet in the solar system? (Jupiter)
2 Which is the smallest planet? (Mercury)
3 What is in the center of the solar system? (sun)
4 What is the main source of light in the solar system? (sun)
5 Which planet supports life? (Earth)
6 Which of the planets has the most moons around it? (Saturn)
7 Which planet is known as the ice giant? (Neptune)
8 Which planet is the red planet? (Mars)
1 Which planet has the most moons? (Saturn)
2 Which planets have the same number of moons? (Mercury, Venus)
3 What is the total number of moons? (205)
4 What is the difference between the number of moons for Jupiter and Neptune? (65)
5 What is the average number of moons for the planets? (25.625)
6 Round the average number of moons in number five to the nearest whole number. (26)

1 What type of force is gravity? A) A force that pushes objects away from each other B) A force that pulls objects toward each other C) A force that only affects objects on Earth Correct Answer: B) A force that pulls objects toward each other

2 Which statement best explains how gravity exerted by a body affects two objects with different masses? A) Gravity pulls equally on both objects, regardless of their masses. B) The object with more mass experiences a stronger gravitational pull. C) Gravity pushes objects with less mass away from th

3 Why do objects fall toward Earth when dropped? Conversation/Discussion Begin the class by taking Learners outside and giving each one a small toy. Ask them to throw their toy as far up into the air as possible on the count of three. Ask Learners these questions to facilitate discussion
1 What happened to the toys? (they fell to the ground)
2 Why did the toys fall to the ground when released? Some will say it is because of something called gravity.
3 Is it a force that we can see? (no it is invisible)

4 Do things ever fall on an angle or only straight down? (straight down) The teacher can then restate that "gravity" is an invisible attractive force that pulls things downward towards the centre of the earth. Ask Learners to jump a few times. Then ask why they always fall back instead of floating o

4 Which of the following best supports the claim that the strength of gravity depends on the mass of the objects? A) A small rock and a large boulder both fall at the same speed. B) The Moon orbits around Earth because of Earth's gravity. C) A person weighs more on Earth than on the Moon because Ear

5 What would happen if Earth's mass decreased by half? A) The gravitational pull on objects would increase. B) The gravitational pull on objects would stay the same. C) The gravitational pull on objects would decrease. Correct Answer: C) The gravitational pull on objects would decrease.

6 Which of the following is an example of gravitational interaction? A) The Moon orbiting around Earth. B) A person pushing a car. C) A balloon floating in the air. Correct Answer: A) The Moon orbiting around Earth. Do you know of any other objects that exert gravity? (Expected responses: sun, moon,

7 Which of the following factors affects the strength of gravitational force between two objects? A) The distance between the objects B) The temperature of the objects C) The speed of the objects Correct Answer: A) The distance between the objects

8 Which of the following explains why astronauts float in space? A) There is no gravity in space. B) Gravity in space pushes them upward. C) They are in free-fall, but Earth's gravity is still pulling them. Correct Answer: C) They are in free-fall, but Earth's gravity is still pulling them.

9 What would happen to the gravitational force between two objects if the distance between them increased? A) The gravitational force would stay the same. B) The gravitational force would increase. C) The gravitational force would decrease. Correct Answer: C) The gravitational force would decrease.

10 Why is the Sun able to keep the planets in orbit around it? A) The Sun's mass creates a strong gravitational force. B) The Sun's heat pulls the planets toward it. C) The Sun's light attracts the planets toward it. Correct Answer: A) The Sun's mass creates a strong gravitational force.
1 What keeps us from floating off into space? (gravity)
2 Who discovered gravity? (Sir Isaac Newton)
3 What does the size of the gravitational pull depend on? (the mass of the object)
4 What happens because of the gravitational pull of the moon? (the tides)

1 When communication satellites are launched into space- why don't they simply fall back to earth? (Communication satellites stay in orbit because they balance the Earth's gravitational pull with their forward motion, ensuring they continually "fall" around the Earth rather than back to its surface.

2 How do you get satellites to orbit at higher altitudes? (you give the satellite more initial velocity to get it further away from the earth before it begins to respond to the earth's gravity and pull it into an orbit)

3 What is a geostationary satellite orbit and why might it help certain regions of the earth benefit from consistent communication? (If the satellite is rocketed to 36,000 kms, it will actually orbit at the same rate as the earth is rotating. This means the satellite will reside over the same region

4 What causes satellites to eventually fall to the earth's surface? (Satellites may encounter increase density of atmosphere that causes drag on the angular velocity of the satellite. Because the ability of the Investigating magnetic pull What do you think will happen to the object as it is moved fu
1 (The force of attraction is greatest, the closer the object is to the magnet and the weakest when the object is furthest away.

2 The force of attraction is greatest, the larger the magnet is. The Sun's Gravity The Sun has the largest mass in our planetary system and therefore possesses a very large gravitational force. This force is enough that the planets are held in orbits, the completion of which, take varying amounts of
1 What are the three parts that make up the earth? (core, mantle, crust)
2 Which is the thinnest layer? (the crust)
3 What is the combination of the upper mantle and crust called? (lithosphere)
4 What are the pieces called that make up the divisions in the lithosphere? (tectonic plates)
5 What is the movement of plates over time called? (plate tectonics or continental drift)
6 How do we know that the continents were once joined? (Duplicate fossils have ben found on each side of the boundaries where the continents were once joined.)
7 What causes the plates to move? (convection currents in the mantle)
8 What are plate margins? (places where the tectonic plates meet)
9 What are three types of processes at the plate margins? (constructive-divergent, destructive-convergent &transform-conservative)
10 In what direction do plates move in a divergent tectonic shift? (plates move apart)
11 What do divergent processes create? (mid-Atlantic ridge)
12 In what direction do plates move in a convergent tectonic shift? (towards each other)
13 What are the two ways that convergent processes create landforms? Sketch a picture) what do the each form?
14 Describe the plate movement in a conservative tectonic shift? (plates slide along each either opposed in direction or alongside each other at different speeds)
15 What types of earth events occur as a result of conservative tectonic shifts? Give an example. (Earthquakes along boundaries fault lines- San Andresa fault California)

2 How do you get satellites to orbit at higher altitudes? (you give the satellite more initial velocity to get it further away from the earth before it begins to respond to the earth's gravity and pull it into an orbit)

3 What is a geostationary satellite orbit and why might it help certain regions of the earth benefit from consistent communication? (If the satellite is rocketed to 36,000 kms, it will actually orbit at the same rate as the earth is rotating. This means the satellite will reside over the same region

4 What causes satellites to eventually fall to the earth's surface? (Satellites may encounter increase density of atmosphere that causes drag on the angular velocity of the satellite. Because the ability of the Investigating magnetic pull What do you think will happen to the object as it is moved fu
1 (The force of attraction is greatest, the closer the object is to the magnet and the weakest when the object is furthest away.

2 The force of attraction is greatest, the larger the magnet is. The Sun's Gravity The Sun has the largest mass in our planetary system and therefore possesses a very large gravitational force. This force is enough that the planets are held in orbits, the completion of which, take varying amounts of
1 What are the three parts that make up the earth? (core, mantle, crust)
2 Which is the thinnest layer? (the crust)
3 What is the combination of the upper mantle and crust called? (lithosphere)
4 What are the pieces called that make up the divisions in the lithosphere? (tectonic plates)
5 What is the movement of plates over time called? (plate tectonics or continental drift)
6 How do we know that the continents were once joined? (Duplicate fossils have ben found on each side of the boundaries where the continents were once joined.)
7 What causes the plates to move? (convection currents in the mantle)
8 What are plate margins? (places where the tectonic plates meet)
9 What are three types of processes at the plate margins? (constructive-divergent, destructive-convergent &transform-conservative)
10 In what direction do plates move in a divergent tectonic shift? (plates move apart)
11 What do divergent processes create? (mid-Atlantic ridge)
12 In what direction do plates move in a convergent tectonic shift? (towards each other)
13 What are the two ways that convergent processes create landforms? Sketch a picture) what do the each form?
14 Describe the plate movement in a conservative tectonic shift? (plates slide along each either opposed in direction or alongside each other at different speeds)
15 What types of earth events occur as a result of conservative tectonic shifts? Give an example. (Earthquakes along boundaries fault lines- San Andresa fault California)

essential_learning_outcomes.elo_description: Develop a model to describe the cycling of water through Earth's systems driven by energy from the sun and the force of gravity
specific_curriculum_outcomes: Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
Refer to grade level expectations at the beginning of this curriculum document.
1 What is the process by which water turns into vapor and rises into the atmosphere? a) Precipitation b) Condensation c) Evaporation d) Runoff
2 What happens to water vapor when it cools down and turns back into liquid? a) Evaporation b) Condensation c) Transpiration d) Precipitation

3 What do we call the water that falls from clouds as rain, snow, sleet, or hail? Disappearing water, what is the trick? The teacher sets a shallow pie pan of water out on the warm windowsill through the day and overnight. In the morning, the water has disappeared. Ask Learners, where did the water
4 What part of the water cycle involves water soaking into the ground? a) Collection b) Infiltration c) Condensation d) Evaporation
5 Which process involves plants releasing water into the air? a) Evaporation b) Precipitation c) Transpiration d) Runoff True or False:
6 Water can never be destroyed or created; it just changes form in the water cycle. a) True b) False
7 Condensation is the process of water turning into vapor. a) True b) False Fill in the Blanks:

8 Water from lakes, rivers, and oceans turns into water vapor through the process of _______________. Have the Learners stand up and pretend their fists are water molecules. Have them simulate cold liquid water molecules by moving their fists slowly back and forth and relatively close together Then
9 The water cycle is a continuous movement of water between the Earth's surface and the _______________.
10 Precipitation can fall as rain, _______________, snow, or hail. Short Answer Questions:
11 What is the difference between evaporation and condensation?
12 Why is the water cycle important for life on Earth?
13 Draw and label the four main stages of the water cycle. (Learners should include: evaporation, condensation, precipitation, and collection.) Answers:
1 c) Evaporation
2 b) Condensation
3 b) Precipitation
4 b) Infiltration
5 c) Transpiration
8 Evaporation

10 Sleet Why does it rain? This is an example of the experiment above. When the water molecules get cold enough, they return from the vapor state to the liquid state. (Have Learners slow the motion of their fists and bring them closer together) These liquid water droplets then gather in clouds. When
11 Evaporation is the process of water turning into vapor, while condensation is the process of vapor cooling and turning back into liquid.
12 The water cycle ensures that water is continuously renewed and distributed, providing fresh water for drinking, agriculture, and maintaining ecosystems.
1 What role does the sun play in the water cycle? (energy provided for water molecules to change state solid>liquid>gas/transpiration in plants)
2 What role does gravity play in the water cycle? (water, ice and snow high in the hills is brought to lower elevations in rivers and streams)
1 What are the water sources for the water cycle? (Rivers, lakes, wells, ponds, runoff from mountains.)
2 Where does the water get the energy to change from liquid to gas state (evaporation)? (sun's energy)
3 Can water evaporate without the sun? (Yes but it is slow, it gathers heat from the surroundings to get enough energy to change state from liquid to gas)

4 Why does cooling in the atmosphere change the vapor back to liquid water? (the particles have less energy, slowdown their motion and get closer together-once they are water particles they cling together, get heavy and fall)
1 Prepare the Base Sphere: Use a large Styrofoam® or cardboard sphere as the base for your model. This represents the Earth.

2 Represent the Geosphere: Landforms: Use modeling clay or play-dough to create mountains, hills, valleys, and other landforms on the surface of the sphere. Labeling: Use markers or colored pencils to label these landforms directly on the sphere as "Geosphere."
3 Depict the Biosphere: Plants and Trees: Place small plastic trees, plants, or figurines on the landforms you created with clay.
4 Create the Hydrosphere: Water Bodies: Use blue cellophane or colored paper to create rivers, lakes, and oceans on the surface of the sphere. Labeling: Label these water bodies as "Hydrosphere."
5 Represent the Atmosphere: Clouds and Sky: Use cotton balls or polyester fiberfill to create clouds around the sphere, representing the atmosphere. Labeling: Label this area as "Atmosphere."

6 Show Interactions: Arrows or Connections: Use toothpicks, thin wire, or string to create arrows between the different components (geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere) to show interactions. For example: o Arrows from the atmosphere (clouds) to the biosphere (plants) to represent rain.

7 Add Labels and Explanations: Use small pieces of paper or cardstock to write labels for each sphere (geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere). Write brief explanations of the interactions and attach them near the corresponding arrows or spheres.

1 Model Accuracy and Detail: Excellent (4): Model accurately depicts the interactions between spheres with detailed and realistic features. Good (3): Model accurately depicts the interactions with some detail. Fair (2): Model depicts interactions with minimal detail and some inaccuracies. Needs Impr

2 Comparison Analysis: Excellent (4): Comparison is thorough, well-explained, and supported by detailed examples. Good (3): Comparison is clear and supported by examples. Fair (2): Comparison is mentioned but lacks depth and supporting examples.

3 Creativity and Presentation: Excellent (4): Model is highly creative, well-organized, and effectively presented. Good (3): Model is creative, organized, and clearly presented. Fair (2): Model shows some creativity and organization but is somewhat unclear. Needs Improvement (1): Model lacks creativ

4 Collaboration and Participation (if group work): Excellent (4): All group members actively participated and collaborated effectively. Good (3): Most group members participated and collaborated effectively. Fair (2): Some group members participated and collaborated. Needs Improvement (1): Few group

3 Canada has sought to access crude oil from what are called "oil sands". What aspects of this process pose dangers to our environment? Learners should consider how transportation of petroleum is a factor here. (teacher sources: https://www.capp.ca/oil/what-are-the- oil-sands/ https://www.watershedc
